channel.title: ghost on T++
channel.description: Recent content in ghost on T++

item.title: Migrating Ghost 0.x to 2.x (and a new domain!)
item.description: To accompany my recent handle change I took the time to upgrade this blog. Previously it had been running Ghost 0.4 on a Digital Ocean CentOS 7 machine. To get from 0.x to 2.x requires that your exported data travel through 1.x first, which is a bit of a challenge. My first attempt was to upgrade the server in place, but issues with *yum *and node got so bad I gave up.

item.title: Self-Hosting Commento alongside Ghost 2.x
item.description: I recently upgraded this blog to Ghost 2.x and took the opportunity to abandon Disqus. It bloated the post page and came with something like 45 tracking scripts/cookies. To replace it I settled on Commento, a small and privacy-focused open source solution. The minimum subscription is $3/month, which is a bit high for a blog that I only pay $5/month for. Luckily they offer a self-hosted option. Here is how I got it setup.

item.title: Setting up Webfaction Sendmail with Ghost
item.description: Ghost’s email guide leans pretty heavily on external services. It doesn’t seem to think most people will want to use the server they are running on. Setting up sendmail on Webfaction is pretty easy, since sendmail is installed on all their boxes. Just put this in your config.js mail: { transport: 'sendmail', fromaddress: 'no-reply@example.com', options: {} }, There is a weird gotcha here though. This didn’t work for me when I left off the options section entirely, and it also failed when I specified the full path to sendmail, as this section encourages you to.
